metadata.summary: Product-Led Growth (PLG) is a go-to-market strategy where the product is the primary vehicle for acquiring, activating, and retaining customers — no sales team required for self-serve tiers.

content.title: PLG Basics and Models
content.summary: Product-Led Growth (PLG) is a go-to-market strategy where the product is the primary vehicle for acquiring, activating, and retaining customers — no sales team required for self-serve tiers. Four models: freemium (forever-free tier with limits), free trial (full access for 7-30 days), open core (free OSS + paid cloud/enterprise), usage-based (pay per consumption unit). Select the model using the 6-factor fit matrix (complexity, decision-maker, price, TTV, trial feasibility, market size); score ≥ 5/6 = pure PLG, 3-4 = hybrid PLG + sales.

why.p: Traditional SaaS sales-led GTM requires linear headcount scaling: more revenue = more sales reps. PLG makes the product the seller for sub-$50K ACV deals: users discover, try, and upgrade without talking to sales. CAC drops from $500-5,000 (sales-led) to $10-100 (PLG). Scale becomes exponential rather than linear. The PLG flywheel — acquisition → activation → engagement → monetization → expansion → referral — compounds with product improvement.

list.item: Choosing a PLG model for a new product or new pricing tier.
Auditing whether a sales-led or marketing-led GTM should adopt or hybridize with PLG.
Onboarding team members who need shared vocabulary (Aha moment, TTV, PLG flywheel, PQL) before deeper PLG work.
Writing strategic memos or pricing-model RFCs that need a defensible model classification with examples.
Tactical optimization (which form fields, which copy) — route to plg-optimization-tactics.
Implementation steps (Aha definition, PQL scoring, playbooks) — route to plg-implementation-guide.
Metric definitions and tracking — route to plg-metrics.
Deep enterprise procurement, six-figure sales-cycle products — methodology explicitly flags ACV greater than $50K as poor PLG fit.

section.name: Four PLG Models with Canonical Examples

rule.statement: Use freemium when the product has low marginal cost per user, network effects, and a mass market TAM where virality can compound. Define upgrade triggers before launching: usage limits, feature gates, user limits, or collaboration needs.
rule.rationale: Without predefined upgrade triggers, freemium free tiers often become permanently free: users have no natural moment to convert because they never hit a wall tied to demonstrated value.

example.note: Slack freemium: 10K message history, 10 integrations, 1:1 video. Aha moment: first channel message with teammates in under 5 minutes. 30%+ teams convert to paid; 143% net revenue retention.
Notion freemium: unlimited personal pages, limited team blocks. Aha moment: first useful page created and shared. Viral loop: templates shared publicly, each view is product exposure.

rule.statement: Use free trial when the product is complex, has clear ROI, or has high switching costs that require time to evaluate. Target trial-to-paid conversion of 15%+ (baseline) or 25%+ (strong). Optimize via free-trial-optimization methodology.
rule.rationale: Free trials work when users need time to experience value but will not experience it on a permanently limited free tier. Complex B2B products with high implementation cost are canonical free-trial cases.

example.note: Zoom freemium + usage-based: unlimited 1:1 calls, 40-minute group limit. Aha moment: first successful video call in under 2 minutes. Growth 10M → 300M daily participants; low CAC through word-of-mouth.

rule.statement: Use open core for developer tools and infrastructure products with a strong self-host community. Choose the license carefully — BSL (Business Source License) and SSPL have materially different community and enterprise adoption effects. Never recommend open core without a license-strategy callout.
rule.rationale: License-shift risk (MongoDB SSPL, HashiCorp BSL) creates community backlash that can permanently reduce enterprise adoption pipeline. License choice is irreversible at scale.

rule.statement: Use usage-based pricing only when a single quantitative value metric exists that aligns with customer perceived value (compute hours, API calls, transactions, queries). Refuse usage-based recommendation if no clean value metric is identifiable.
rule.rationale: Usage-based pricing without a clear value metric creates unpredictable billing that confuses customers. The metric must feel fair and proportional to value received.

example.note: Calendly freemium: 1 event type free → multiple event types + team scheduling on paid. Viral loop: every meeting booked exposes recipients to the product ("Powered by Calendly"). TTV: 3 minutes to first booking.

section.name: PLG Fit Matrix, Flywheel, and Hybrid Motion

rule.statement: Before recommending a PLG model, score the product against all 6 factors: complexity (simple/intuitive = 1), decision-maker (end user = 1), price (under $50K/year = 1), TTV (minutes to hours = 1), trial feasibility (easy to try = 1), market size (large TAM = 1). Score 5-6 = pure PLG; 3-4 = hybrid PLG + sales; below 3 = sales-led with PLG signals only.
rule.rationale: Skipping the matrix leads LLMs to default-recommend freemium for almost any SaaS product regardless of fit. The matrix forces explicit checks against the documented failure modes.

rule.statement: For hybrid PLG + sales, use ACV $1K/month as the cutoff: below = self-serve closes the loop; above = sales-assist motion with PQL handoff to an SDR. Make this threshold explicit in every hybrid recommendation.
rule.rationale: The PLG-vs-Sales-Led comparison table flattens product-led-sales (PLS) reality. Most modern PLG at $1K-$50K ACV needs a sales-assist motion that pure PLG analysis misses.

rule.statement: Treat the PLG flywheel (acquisition → activation → engagement → monetization → expansion → referral) as a mental model for explaining the growth loop, not as a measurement framework. Route to plg-metrics for actual instrumentation and KPI definitions.
rule.rationale: The flywheel diagram is directionally correct but is not a pipeline with measurable handoffs. Presenting it as a metric framework produces unmeasurable "stage health" reporting that does not drive decisions.

antipattern.description: LLM recommends freemium for every SaaS product without running the fit matrix.
antipattern.reason: Freemium fails for complex products, high-ACV products, and products where decision-makers are not end users. The fit matrix walk-through is mandatory before model recommendation.

antipattern.description: Extrapolating "Slack did X, therefore we should do X" without checking for network effects, viral coefficient, and market distribution differences.
antipattern.reason: Canonical examples (Slack, Zoom, Notion) are survivor-biased mega-cases with distribution dynamics most products cannot replicate. Treat them as anchor points only; replace with peer comps in actual recommendations.

antipattern.description: Recommending open core without naming the license and its downstream adoption risk.
antipattern.reason: License-shift risk (BSL, SSPL) is irreversible at scale and permanently affects enterprise pipeline. Cannot be omitted from an open-core recommendation.

list.item: Comparison matrix is directional, not predictive: a "Good Fit" product can still fail PLG if onboarding is broken; the methodology underweights team capability.
The PLG-vs-Sales-Led table flattens hybrid PLS (product-led-sales) reality — most modern PLG ($1K–$50K ACV) needs a sales-assist motion not deeply covered in this methodology.
CAC bands ("$10–$100 PLG") are aggregate and reflect category leaders; B2B PLG typically lands $200–$800.

Examples (Slack, Zoom, Notion) are survivor-biased mega-cases. Agents must avoid extrapolating "X did Y, therefore we should" without checking distribution dynamics, network effects, and viral coefficient.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: This is the foundational/strategy layer of the PLG knowledge cluster. A single Claude subagent typically reads this methodology to produce a model-fit memo: it ingests product fundamentals (price, ICP, complexity, decision-maker, TTV target), maps them to the four models, and outputs a recommendation with rationale. For execution, the orchestrator then chains to plg-implementation-guide (steps), plg-optimization-tactics (tactics), and plg-metrics (instrumentation). Output stays advisory; no autonomous mutations.

Recommended subagents: plg-model-advisor (reads this methodology, takes product fundamentals as input, returns a ranked model recommendation with rationale). plg-fit-scorer (scores a product against the "Is PLG Right for You?" matrix, returning a 0–6 fit score and gap list). Hand-off to faion-growth-agent for downstream tactical work.

list.item: posthog / mixpanel / amplitude — Measure baseline TTV and activation rate to validate model choice
stripe CLI — Inspect existing pricing model, prototype new tiers in test mode
chargebee / paddle API — Multi-tier subscription experiments, freemium-to-paid metering
lago (OSS metering) — Open-source usage-based billing engine for Model 4
openllmetry — Trace LLM-driven onboarding flows and TTV instrumentation
Stripe Billing — Required for any of the four models
Paddle — Merchant of record; better for global SaaS PLG
Chargebee — Strong for hybrid (PLG + sales-assist) tiering
Lago — Usage-based metering with self-hosted control
OpenView Insights — Benchmark provider; agent should cite, not invent
Reforge / Lenny's Newsletter — Strategy reference; quality varies by recency
Always derive Aha moment from cohort data of converted users, not from product team intuition (interview converted users + cohort analysis).
Use this methodology as a strategy anchor only — do not recommend a model without explicit TTV, ACV, and ICP inputs from the operator.
For hybrid recommendations, make the ACV cutoff explicit ($1K/mo); below = self-serve, above = sales-assist.
When recommending freemium, force the agent to also propose the upgrade trigger (limit, feature gate, time, collaboration) and the corresponding metric to track.
Pair every model recommendation with a "PLG kill criteria" — if activation rate after 90 days less than X% or freemium → paid less than Y%, switch model.
Treat the four mega-examples as anchor points only; replace with peer comps in the actual recommendation.
LLMs will default to recommending "freemium" for almost any SaaS — pin the prompt to require the fit matrix walk-through before the recommendation.
This methodology does not deeply cover product-led-sales (PLS) — agents will under-recommend the hybrid motion. Add explicit PLS branch in the advisor prompt for ACV $1K–$10K/mo.
Open-source / Open Core advice underweights ToS, license-shift risk (BSL, SSPL); agents must NOT recommend open-core without a license-strategy callout.
Usage-based pricing requires a clean value metric — agent should refuse the recommendation if the input cannot identify one quantitative unit of value.
The "PLG Flywheel" diagram is a mental model, not a measurement framework; agents should never present it as a metric pipeline. Route to plg-metrics for actual instrumentation.
Do not generate marketing copy from this methodology — it is strategic taxonomy. Route to growth-conversion-optimization or growth-landing-page-design.

template.purpose: Dataclass scoring product against 6-factor fit matrix; returns fit score and pure_plg / hybrid / sales_led recommendation.
